explicit_text: I was born and spent the first 16 years of my life in Nepal, living in a refugee camp with my family. Our life there was very hard, as we lived in fragile huts without basic necessities like food, electricity, or proper homes. Nepal was a place where many Bhutanese-Nepali refugees like us stayed for decades, but we were not accepted as citizens. Despite the hardships, I had a supportive community and friends who spoke the same language as me. It was in Nepal that we awaited resettlement to other countries through organizations like the International Organization for Migration.
implicit_text: I was born and spent the first 16 years of my life in a South Asian country, living in a refugee camp with my family. Our life there was very hard, as we lived in fragile huts without basic necessities like food, electricity, or proper homes. This country was a place where many Bhutanese-Nepali refugees like us stayed for decades, but we were not accepted as citizens. Despite the hardships, I had a supportive community and friends who spoke the same language as me. It was there that we awaited resettlement to other countries through organizations like the International Organization for Migration.

explicit_text: My parents and grandparents were born in Bhutan, a landlocked Himalayan kingdom ruled by kings since 1907. In the 1980s, Bhutan introduced harsh citizenship laws targeting people of Nepali origin like my family, requiring knowledge of Dzongkha and Bhutanese customs. This led to cultural cleansing and persecution, forcing many, including my family, to flee to Nepal. Though Bhutan was our motherland, the government’s actions made it impossible for us to stay. The violence and discrimination deeply affected our community and shaped our refugee journey.
implicit_text: My parents and grandparents were born in a landlocked Himalayan kingdom ruled by kings since 1907. In the 1980s, this kingdom introduced harsh citizenship laws targeting people of Nepali origin like my family, requiring knowledge of the national language and customs. This led to cultural cleansing and persecution, forcing many, including my family, to flee to a neighboring country. Though this kingdom was our motherland, the government’s actions made it impossible for us to stay. The violence and discrimination deeply affected our community and shaped our refugee journey.

explicit_text: My family and I moved to Saint Paul, Minnesota on December 9th, 2016, after leaving Nepal. It became our final destination in the United States, where we were welcomed by members of the Blue Cross Society. Living in Saint Paul, I faced challenges adapting to a new language and culture, especially at school, but with hard work and support, I improved. Saint Paul represents a new beginning for me, a place where I could continue my education and build a better future for my family.

This city represents a new beginning for me, a place where I could continue my education and build a better future for my family.

explicit_text: The United States became my new home when my family resettled here in December 2016 after years as refugees in Nepal. Moving here meant adapting to a new language, culture, and environment, which was tough at first, especially in school. However, the USA offered opportunities I had never seen before, like better education, healthcare, and modern technology. Living here with my family and community has been a journey of resilience and hope, and I am grateful for the chance to build a brighter future.

explicit_text: The UNHCR played a crucial role in our lives when we were refugees in Nepal by providing food rations and establishing camps for Bhutanese refugees like my family. Without their support, survival would have been even more difficult in the harsh conditions we faced. Their assistance gave us a place to live and helped sustain our community during those challenging years. I am thankful for the help they provided during that painful time.

explicit_text: The International Organization for Migration was instrumental in helping my family and many others resettle from Nepal to countries like the USA, Canada, and Norway. They facilitated our journey to places where we could access education, electricity, and better living standards. Thanks to IOM, my family was able to make the difficult decision to move to the United States in search of a better life. Their work gave us hope and a pathway to new opportunities.

explicit_text: Dzongkha is the official language of Bhutan and became a requirement for citizenship under the 1980 laws imposed by the Bhutanese government. My parents and grandparents, who are of Nepali origin, did not speak Dzongkha and instead had their own languages and customs. This language requirement was used as a tool of exclusion and persecution against our community. It was one of the reasons my family was forced to flee Bhutan and live as refugees in Nepal.

It was one of the reasons my family was forced to flee that kingdom and live as refugees in a neighboring country.

explicit_text: The Kingdom of Bhutan is where my family’s roots lie, ruled by kings since 1907. However, in the 1980s, the kingdom introduced strict citizenship laws that targeted the Nepali-origin population, including my parents and grandparents. This led to cultural cleansing, persecution, and forced displacement of many Bhutanese-Nepali people. Though Bhutan was our homeland, the political climate forced us to flee and seek refuge in Nepal.

Their assistance made the difficult adjustment period easier as we adapted to a new culture and environment. I am grateful for their kindness and support during that time.

explicit_text: Our journey to the United States began with a brief stay in Miami, Florida, where we stayed at a hotel for one day after arriving on December 8th, 2016. Miami was our first stop before moving on to our final destination in Saint Paul, Minnesota. It was a mix of excitement and fear as I traveled by plane for the first time and faced a new environment. Miami marked the beginning of our new life in America.

explicit_text: Canada was one of the countries where the International Organization for Migration resettled Bhutanese-Nepali refugees like my family. Although we did not move there ourselves, Canada represented a place of opportunity and better living conditions compared to the refugee camps in Nepal. It was part of the broader resettlement options offered to us by international agencies. Knowing that countries like Canada welcomed refugees gave us hope for a better future.

explicit_text: Norway was another country where Bhutanese-Nepali refugees were resettled through the International Organization for Migration. Like Canada and the USA, Norway offered access to education, electricity, and modern facilities that we never had in the refugee camps. Although my family chose to move to the United States, Norway symbolized the international support available to displaced Bhutanese-Nepali people. It was part of the network of countries providing new opportunities for refugees.
